one_line_takeaway: A 2024 survey that reframes machine unlearning through a two-part taxonomy of its OWN privacy risks, arguing that the learned-vs-unlearned model gap turns a right-to-be-forgotten mechanism into a new attack surface (MIA, attribute/inversion/extraction, plus backdoor/poisoning via malicious unlearning requests).
key_finding: The central synthesized finding is that machine unlearning has a 'secondary privacy leakage' problem: nearly all surveyed data-oriented and model-oriented techniques trade unlearning efficiency against a strong retraining-equivalent guarantee, and the residual discrepancy between the learned and unlearned models is exactly what adversaries exploit. The paper maps this into concrete regularities: (a) data-partition methods (e.g. SISA and its graph descendants) are most exposed to MEMBERSHIP INFERENCE because retrained submodels perturb the aggregated confidence vectors; (b) data-modification methods (statistical-query/summation transforms, class-unlearning noise) are most exposed to ATTRIBUTE INFERENCE; (c) model-reset methods (certified removal / influence-function / Fisher-information) are most exposed to POISONING (the slow-down attack that forces retraining); (d) model-modification methods that precompute/store parameters (trees, GANs) are more exposed to MODEL INVERSION under white-box access. It also flags that model-extraction against unlearning (e.g. Jang et al. on LLM unlearning) is still largely an evaluation metric rather than a practical attack, and identifies verifiability + robustness testing as an under-addressed gap.
core_contribution: A survey (not an empirical paper) whose own contribution is an organizing framework, not any new algorithm. The authors contribute (1) a taxonomy of machine-unlearning TECHNIQUES with two top-level branches -- data-oriented (subdivided into data partition and data modification) and model-oriented (subdivided into model reset and model modification); and (2) a parallel taxonomy of PRIVACY THREATS specific to unlearning, split into 'information-stealing attacks' (membership inference, attribute inference, model inversion, model extraction) and 'model-breaking attacks' (backdoor, poisoning). Its distinguishing claim versus prior unlearning surveys is that it frames the field from the perspective of the *secondary* privacy leakage that unlearning itself introduces -- i.e. that an operation whose purpose is to protect privacy (the 'right to be forgotten' under GDPR/CCPA) can itself leak information about the forgotten data because the learned and unlearned models differ. The paper maps each surveyed unlearning method to the attack class it is most vulnerable to, and collects candidate defenses/verification metrics. Note: the individual algorithms it discusses (SISA, Certified Removal, GraphEraser, DaRE, DeltaBoost, PRU, Fisher/influence-function methods, GAN unlearning, etc.) are CITED prior work being organized, not this paper's own inventions.
threat_model: The survey's unifying threat model is the 'two-model discrepancy' adversary: because unlearning produces a learned model M_o and an unlearned model M_u, an attacker who can query both (their posteriors, parameters, or gradients) can exploit the difference between them to recover information about the forgotten data D_f -- attack targets in unlearning are the unlearned DATA, the learned model, and the unlearned model, which differs from classic ML privacy where the target is the training data / trained model. Information-stealing threats (membership/attribute inference, inversion, extraction) assume the attacker constructs shadow models and combines the M_o and M_u posteriors into a feature vector for a binary attack classifier (Chen et al.'s MIA-on-unlearning being the seminal instance). Model-breaking threats assume an attacker who issues malicious unlearning REQUESTS: injecting backdoor/poison samples then requesting their deletion to trigger the backdoor (Qian et al.; BAMU by Zhang et al.; camouflaged poisoning by Di et al.), or poisoning to inflate approximation error so retraining is triggered repeatedly (Marchant et al.'s 'slow-down' attack on certified removal). The paper explicitly notes it includes poisoning -- normally a security/integrity attack -- under 'privacy' because in the unlearning setting the induced leakage/degradation is treated as a privacy risk.
limitations: As the paper itself concedes at several points: (1) many vulnerability assignments are the authors' inferences, not demonstrated -- Table 3/4 vulnerability entries are asterisked as 'potential', and the paper explicitly writes that whether the M_o/M_u representation difference for an attribute can actually extract D_f's private info 'still needs to be studied'. (2) It is a qualitative survey with no experimental validation, no unified benchmark, and no numeric comparison, so the accuracy/effectiveness/efficiency ratings are coarse binary judgments. (3) Coverage skews toward classification/GAN/GNN vintage (2019-2023) work; LLM unlearning is touched only lightly (one extraction example). (4) The 'privacy' framing is stretched to absorb integrity attacks (backdoor, poisoning), which the authors acknowledge is a definitional choice. (5) Defenses are discussed thinly relative to attacks. (6) Metadata artifacts (ACM template dated 2018, placeholder DOI) indicate a preprint; the arXiv version is v1, June 2024.

quantitative_results: No first-party quantitative experiments: this is a narrative survey and reports no numeric results of its own.

Comparison Tables 3 and 4 rate cited methods only qualitatively (checkmark/cross) on Accuracy, Effectiveness, and Efficiency, plus an (often asterisked = inferred) privacy-vulnerability label; no accuracy percentages, runtimes, or attack success rates are given.

Only complexity figures quoted are from cited methods, e.g. Izzo et al.'s Projective Residual Update having O(d) cost linear in feature dimension d (attributed to reference [37], not measured here); Graves et al.'s inversion applying a PROCESS step every g in [500,1000] gradient-descent steps (attributed to [64]).
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines. The survey ORGANIZES (does not benchmark) representative cited techniques, grouped by its taxonomy:

Data partition: SISA (Bourtoule et al. [10]), DC-k-means (Ginart et al. [21]), adaptive-removal SISA variant (Gupta et al. [25]), GraphEraser (Chen et al. [28]), guided inductive graph unlearning (Wang et al. [29]).
Data modification: statistical-query/summation unlearning (Cao et al. [8]), attribute unlearning (Guo et al. [31]), error-maximizing-noise class unlearning (Tarun et al. [32]).

Model reset: Certified Removal (Guo et al. [11]), PRU (Izzo et al. [37]), distributed noisy GD (Neel et al. [39]), noisy-GD deletion (Chourasia et al. [40]), approximate graph unlearning (Chien et al. [41/58]), certified edge unlearning (Wu et al. [42]), Fisher-information selective forgetting and ML-Forgetting (Golatkar et al. [44,45]), feature/label removal (Warnecke et al. [46]).

Model modification: DaRE forest (Brophy et al. [13]), DeltaBoost GBDT (Wu et al. [47]), student-teacher unlearning (Chundawat et al. [48]), zero-shot Gated Knowledge Transfer (Chundawat et al. [49]), linear-filtration class unlearning (Baumhauer et al. [50]), GAN unlearning (Kong et al. [53], Bae et al. [54], Moon et al. [56], Sun et al. [57]).

Attacks surveyed as evaluation lenses: MIA-on-unlearning (Chen et al. [12]), deletion inference/reconstruction (Gao et al. [60]), attribute inference in feature-level unlearning (Stock et al. [63]), model inversion on class-level unlearning (Graves et al. [64], Baumhauer [50]), LLM model extraction (Jang et al. [66]), malicious/backdoor unlearning (Qian et al. [69], Hu et al. [18], BAMU Zhang et al. [19], Di et al. [71]), slow-down poisoning (Marchant et al. [20]).
